* CEO sets phase lock via ceo_memory update — only CEO can advance the gate
* Default: phase 0 (current state)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Migration adds `phase` column to public.tasks (numeric, nullable for legacy rows, default 0)
- [ ] ceo_phase_lock readable from ceo_memory key OR dedicated phase_lock table
- [ ] bd ready filters `phase <= current_phase_max` — verified by inserting a synthetic Tier-2 task and confirming it does not surface when lock = 0
- [ ] bd claim rejects Tier-2 task with explanatory error when lock = 0 (verbatim test output)
- [ ] bd claim accepts same Tier-2 task after CEO advances lock to 2 (verbatim test output)
- [ ] Backfill: assign `phase` to existing tasks based on KEI tier mapping (Phase 0 = memory KEIs, Phase 0.5 = stability KEIs, etc — see KEI-117 for the canonical mapping)
- [ ] Anti-false-complete CI gate (KEI-108) PASS on the PR — installer for any new service file, etc

## Why this is Phase 0

Prevents the sequencing violation that caused Tier-0 infrastructure to be built on top of incomplete memory. Must ship BEFORE Phase 0 closes so Phase 0.5 onward is mechanically enforced.

## Ownership

Assigned to Max. Scout is research-only per IDENTITY (would not build).

## Source

Dave KEI-118 (Linear) ratified 2026-05-17 \~07:00 UTC.

title: KEI-100 — Fix Linear/Supabase ID misalignment: Linear is the only source of task IDs, reconcile existing rows
description:
## The Problem

Linear and Supabase tasks use completely different ID spaces. "[KEI-77](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-77/kei-84-bidirectional-linear-supabase-sync-linear-webhook-supabase)" in Linear = bidirectional sync. "[KEI-77](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-77/kei-84-bidirectional-linear-supabase-sync-linear-webhook-supabase)" in Supabase = Cognee write-path recovery. The bidirectional sync webhook fires, finds a Supabase row with the same ID, and updates the wrong task. Status changes propagate to the wrong rows. The board is corrupted.

Root causes:

1. Auto-KEI ([KEI-18](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-18/kei-18-openclaw-auto-creates-supabase-task-on-every-ceo-directive)) creates Supabase rows directly with its own sequential IDs — not derived from Linear
2. CEO manual seeding inserted rows with arbitrary IDs ("[KEI-71](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-71/t01-create-keiracomdispatcher-repo-build-claude-code-image)", "[KEI-73](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-73/t02-install-litellm-on-vultr-sydney)" etc.) that conflict with existing Supabase rows or future Linear IDs
3. The Linear→Supabase webhook (KEI-84) assumes IDs are shared — they are not
4. Issue titles say "KEI-99" but Linear assigns "[KEI-83](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-83/kei-99-relay-watcher-session-name-resilience-hardcoded-session-names)" as the actual identifier — title and ID diverge

## The Fix — Three Parts

### Part 1: Linear is the ONLY source of task IDs

Going forward, no task row is ever created in Supabase except via the Linear webhook. No manual seeding. No direct CEO inserts. No auto-KEI direct Supabase writes.

Auto-KEI ([KEI-18](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-18/kei-18-openclaw-auto-creates-supabase-task-on-every-ceo-directive)) must be updated to:

1. Create the issue in Linear (getting Linear's real identifier)
2. Let the Linear→Supabase webhook create the Supabase row with the correct ID
3. Stop writing directly to Supabase tasks table

### Part 2: Reconcile existing misaligned rows

Run a reconciliation:

1. Pull all open issues from Linear via API
2. For each Linear issue: upsert into Supabase tasks using the LINEAR identifier as `id`
3. Mark any Supabase rows whose IDs don't exist in Linear as `done` (they're noise artifacts)
4. Result: Supabase tasks table is a clean mirror of Linear open issues

### Part 3: Enforce title alignment

When filing a KEI in Linear, the title must start with the Linear identifier itself. The system will know what number it gets after creation. Update the naming convention:

* File in Linear → get Linear ID (e.g., [KEI-83](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-83/kei-99-relay-watcher-session-name-resilience-hardcoded-session-names))
* Title is simply: "Relay watcher session name resilience"
* No manual KEI number in the title — Linear's own ID IS the KEI number

Add a Linear webhook validation that rejects titles starting with "KEI-XX" where XX doesn't match the Linear identifier.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Auto-KEI no longer writes directly to Supabase — creates in Linear, webhook syncs
- [ ] Reconciliation query run: Supabase tasks mirror Linear open issues exactly
- [ ] No Supabase task row exists with an ID that doesn't match a Linear issue identifier
- [ ] Test: create a new issue in Linear → verify Supabase row appears within 5 seconds with matching ID
- [ ] Test: update Linear issue status → Supabase row updates correctly (no wrong-row side effects)
- [ ] Verbatim diff showing Supabase tasks vs Linear open issues = zero mismatches

title: KEI-99 — Relay watcher session name resilience: hardcoded session names cause silent message drops
description:
## The Bug — Confirmed in Production 2026-05-17

When the server restarted for the RAM upgrade, Dave manually started Elliot's session with the name `elliot`. The relay watcher was hardcoded to deliver messages to `elliottbot:0.0`. Session name mismatch → silent send-keys drop. Four CEO messages were found stuck in the `processed/` folder, never delivered to Elliot's pane.

This is the same class of fragility as all the other Model A problems: a hardcoded value that silently fails when reality doesn't match the constant.

## Fix

The relay watcher must not rely on a single hardcoded session name per agent. It should:

1. Maintain a priority list of known session names per callsign:

```
elliot: ["elliottbot", "elliot", "elliot-agent"]
aiden:  ["aiden", "aiden-agent"]
max:    ["max", "max-agent"]
# etc.
```

2. On message delivery, attempt each name in order via `tmux send-keys -t {name}`. First successful hit wins.
3. If all names fail → post to #ceo: `[SYSTEM] [CALLSIGN] delivery failed — no active session found under known names. Manual intervention required.`
4. On successful delivery to a non-primary name → log the working name and promote it to primary for that session.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Relay watcher config updated with priority name list per agent
- [ ] Delivery loop tries all known names before failing
- [ ] Failed delivery fires #ceo alert (Elliot only per access rule)
- [ ] Successful delivery to non-primary name logs and promotes that name
- [ ] Test: start a session named `elliot` (not `elliottbot`), send a message, confirm delivery succeeds
notes: PR #907 open — https://github.com/Keiracom/Agency_OS/pull/907. Awaiting dual approval (Elliot+Max). All 5 acceptance criteria met; smoke verified empirically. Out-of-scope follow-up: src/relay/relay_consumer.py has same pattern, file separate KEI when Phase 2 lands.

title: KEI-97 — Zombie task detection: agent heartbeats + liveness check so CEO check doesn't report dead agents as active
description:
## The Problem

When agents crash, Supabase tasks remain in `active` status with `claimed_by` populated. There is no liveness signal. The CEO layer reads `active` tasks and reports them as in-progress — but agents may be dead. This produced false confidence reports for over an hour during the post-restart period.

## What's Needed

**1. Agent heartbeat writes**
Every active agent writes a heartbeat row to a `task_heartbeats` table every 5 minutes while a task is claimed. Schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE task_heartbeats (
  id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() PRIMARY KEY,
  task_id TEXT REFERENCES tasks(id),
  agent TEXT NOT NULL,
  written_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

**2. Zombie task detection trigger**
A Supabase function (callable by CEO and by a cron) that identifies zombie tasks:

```sql
SELECT t.id, t.claimed_by, t.updated_at,
       MAX(h.written_at) as last_heartbeat
FROM tasks t
LEFT JOIN task_heartbeats h ON h.task_id = t.id
WHERE t.status = 'active'
GROUP BY t.id
HAVING MAX(h.written_at) < NOW() - INTERVAL '10 minutes'
   OR MAX(h.written_at) IS NULL;
```

If a task is active but has no heartbeat in 10 minutes → it is a zombie. Agent is dead or stuck.

**3. CEO check protocol update**
When CEO runs a "check," the protocol must include:

* Supabase active tasks (what is claimed)
* task_heartbeats recency (is the agent actually alive)
* Slack #execution recent activity (last post per agent)
* If any active task has no heartbeat in 10 mins → report as ZOMBIE, not in-progress

**4. Auto-reset zombies**
A trigger that resets zombie tasks to `available` after 15 minutes with no heartbeat, so they can be reclaimed by a live agent.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] task_heartbeats table exists
- [ ] Each agent writes heartbeat every 5 minutes during active task
- [ ] CEO `check` query surfaces zombie tasks (active + no recent heartbeat) separately from genuinely active tasks
- [ ] Zombie tasks auto-reset to available after 15 min no-heartbeat
- [ ] Verbatim query output showing heartbeat recency per active task

title: KEI-95 — Relay reconnection on reboot: systemd dependency ordering + loginctl linger (Model A fix while Dispatcher builds)
description:
## The Problem

On server reboot, the bidirectional OpenClaw relay does not re-establish automatically. Elliot starts (Claude Code is running in tmux) but three relay directions are broken:

* Inbound: Slack → Elliot (directives don't reach him)
* Outbound: Elliot → Slack (posts don't reach #ceo)
* Supabase polling loop: not starting

Root cause: no systemd dependency ordering. elliot-agent.service starts concurrently with openclaw.service. If Claude Code initialises before OpenClaw is healthy, MCP bridge connections fail silently and the agent runs deaf and mute.

## Why This Is A Model A Problem Only

The Dispatcher (Model B) eliminates this entirely. Ephemeral containers do not own relay connections. The Dispatcher is the single always-on relay. Containers receive a scoped JWT + task, execute, write to Supabase, and terminate. The Dispatcher reads output and posts to Slack. No per-agent relay. Nothing to reconnect on reboot.

This KEI fixes the current persistent agent model while the Dispatcher is being built.

## Fix

1. Add systemd dependency to all 6 agent service files:

```
[Unit]
Requires=openclaw.service
After=openclaw.service
```

2. Add health check to openclaw.service ExecStartPost — confirm relay is accepting connections before agents start.
3. Add `loginctl enable-linger elliotbot` to ensure user services start on boot without requiring an active login session. Run this once on the server.
4. Verify startup order: `systemctl --user list-dependencies elliot-agent.service` — openclaw should appear as a dependency.
5. Test: reboot server, confirm all 6 sessions start AND Elliot posts fleet status to #ceo without manual intervention.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 6 agent service files updated with Requires=openclaw + After=openclaw
- [ ] `loginctl enable-linger elliotbot` confirmed active
- [ ] Server reboot test: all 6 agents post \[SYSTEM\] to correct channels automatically
- [ ] Elliot posts fleet status to #ceo within 60 seconds of boot — no manual intervention
- [ ] Inbound relay confirmed: post a message to #ceo, Elliot responds
- [ ] Verbatim `tmux ls` output after reboot showing all 6 sessions

title: KEI-94 — Systemd agent services: Claude Code exits on boot, destroying tmux sessions — fix with Restart=always + keep-alive wrapper
description:
## What Happened

KEI-86 built systemd services with ExecStart launching Claude Code in a detached tmux session. On server restart, the ExecStartPost Slack curl fired correctly (confirming systemd ran), but Claude Code exited after boot, destroying the tmux sessions with it. `tmux ls` shows no sessions. Agents are dead.

## Root Cause

`ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'tmux new-session -d -s {callsign} "claude" || tmux attach-session -t {callsign}'`

This creates a detached tmux session and the bash process exits immediately (correct for Type=forking). But Claude Code itself exits when it has no pending work or interactive input — destroying the tmux session. Systemd considers the service "started" (the forking succeeded) but does not track the tmux session's lifecycle. Session dies silently.

## Fix Required

1. Change service Type from `forking` to `simple`
2. Add `Restart=always` with `RestartSec=10` so systemd respawns if Claude Code exits
3. Wrap the ExecStart in a keep-alive loop that respawns Claude Code within the tmux session if it exits:

```bash
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'tmux new-session -d -s {callsign} bash -c "while true; do claude --dangerously-skip-permissions; sleep 5; done"'
```

This means: if Claude Code exits for any reason, it restarts inside the same tmux session automatically. The session stays alive permanently.

4. Update all 6 service files
5. `systemctl --user daemon-reload`
6. `systemctl --user restart` all 6 agents
7. Verify with `tmux ls` — should show 6 persistent sessions

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 6 service files updated with keep-alive loop
- [ ] `tmux ls` shows 6 sessions after `systemctl --user restart` for all agents
- [ ] Kill Claude Code manually inside one tmux session — confirm it respawns within 10 seconds
- [ ] Server reboot test: confirm all 6 sessions exist after reboot without manual intervention
- [ ] Verbatim `tmux ls` output pasted as evidence
notes: Dispatched to atlas 2026-05-17 ~01:46 UTC via /tmp/telegram-relay-atlas/inbox/kei94_amendment_1778982400.json. Step 0 PRE-CONFIRMED (Dave authorized direct via #ceo). Awaiting atlas Step 0 RESTATE + PR.

title: KEI-86 — Systemd agent services + ExecStartPost #ceo relay: close KEI-43 properly and enable mechanical restart notifications
description:
## Context

[KEI-43](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-43/kei-43-agent-auto-start-systemd-services-boot-recovery-zero-dave-touch) (agent auto-start systemd services) was marked Done in Linear but the install never ran on the live server. Agents run in tmux only — no systemd wrapper. On server reboot all 6 sessions die and nothing restarts them.

This KEI closes [KEI-43](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-43/kei-43-agent-auto-start-systemd-services-boot-recovery-zero-dave-touch) and KEI-85 in one PR — Option C ratified by CEO.

## Access rule — CRITICAL

Only Elliot has write access to #ceo (C0B2PM3TV0B). No other agent posts there directly.

## What to build

### Agents 2–6 (aiden, max, atlas, orion, scout)

ExecStartPost posts \[SYSTEM\] restart notification to **#execution** (C0B3QB0K1GQ) only.

```ini
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c 'sleep 15 && curl -s -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${SLACK_BOT_TOKEN}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"channel\":\"C0B3QB0K1GQ\",\"text\":\"[SYSTEM] [{CALLSIGN}] session online.\"}"'
```

### Elliot only

ExecStartPost posts to **#ceo** (C0B2PM3TV0B) with a full status summary — confirming Elliot is up AND reporting health of all other services.

```ini
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c 'sleep 20 && \
  STATUS=$(systemctl --user is-active aiden-agent max-agent atlas-agent orion-agent scout-agent 2>&1) && \
  curl -s -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${SLACK_BOT_TOKEN}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"channel\":\"C0B2PM3TV0B\",\"text\":\"[SYSTEM] [ELLIOT] Server online. Agent fleet status:\n${STATUS}\"}"'
```

## Steps

1. Write all 6 service files to `~/.config/systemd/user/`
2. `systemctl --user daemon-reload`
3. `systemctl --user enable` all 6
4. **Live test before PR:** restart elliot-agent, confirm \[SYSTEM\] post appears in #ceo within 25 seconds
5. Restart one clone agent, confirm \[SYSTEM\] post appears in #execution only — NOT #ceo
6. Open PR

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Elliot restart → \[SYSTEM\] post in #ceo within 25 seconds — verbatim shown
- [ ] Clone agent restart → \[SYSTEM\] post in #execution only, nothing in #ceo — verbatim shown
- [ ] All 6 services active (running) — verbatim systemctl list-units output
- [ ] PR opened with all 6 service files

title: KEI-85 — systemd ExecStartPost: mechanical Slack post to #ceo on every agent restart
description:
## Problem

When the server restarts, agents come back online via systemd auto-start ([KEI-43](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-43/kei-43-agent-auto-start-systemd-services-boot-recovery-zero-dave-touch)). Nothing mechanically notifies Dave or #ceo that an agent is back up. It relies on Elliot remembering to post. Discipline-based. Breaks under pressure.

## Fix

Add `ExecStartPost` to each of the 6 agent systemd service files. The moment a session starts, before any task claiming, a Slack API call fires directly to #ceo.

## Implementation

In each agent's `.service` file, add after the `ExecStart` line:

```
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c 'sleep 10 && curl -s -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SLACK_BOT_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"channel\":\"C0B2PM3TV0B\",\"text\":\"[SYSTEM] [CALLSIGN] agent session started. Server online.\"}"'
```

Sleep 10 seconds to allow session to stabilise before posting. Each agent uses its own callsign in the message. SLACK_BOT_TOKEN must be available in the systemd environment.

## Six service files to update

* elliot-agent.service
* aiden-agent.service
* max-agent.service
* atlas-agent.service
* orion-agent.service
* scout-agent.service

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 6 service files updated with ExecStartPost
- [ ] `systemctl --user daemon-reload` run after changes
- [ ] Test: restart one agent service manually, confirm \[SYSTEM\] post appears in #ceo within 15 seconds
- [ ] Verbatim #ceo Slack message shown as evidence

title: KEI-84 — Bidirectional Linear ↔ Supabase sync: Linear webhook → Supabase tasks table
description:
## The Bug

Linear and Supabase tasks are not bidirectionally synced. The existing sync ([KEI-74](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-74/t03-cloudflare-tunnel-zero-trust-on-vultr)) only goes Supabase → Linear. The reverse direction — Linear issue created/updated → Supabase tasks row created/updated — does not exist.

Result: every KEI filed in Linear is invisible to agents until manually seeded into Supabase. Agents run `bd ready`, get empty queue, post READY loops. Dave has to manually INSERT rows to unblock the team.

## Required Fix: Linear Webhook → Supabase

Register a Linear webhook on the Keiracom workspace for all issue events. On receipt, upsert into the Supabase tasks table.

### Status mapping (Linear → Supabase)

```
Backlog      → available
Todo         → available
In Progress  → active
In Review    → active
Done         → done
Cancelled    → cancelled
Duplicate    → cancelled
```

### On issue CREATED

```sql
INSERT INTO tasks (id, title, status, priority)
VALUES (issue.identifier, issue.title, mapped_status, issue.priority)
ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING;
```

### On issue UPDATED (status change)

```sql
UPDATE tasks SET status = mapped_status, updated_at = now()
WHERE id = issue.identifier
AND status != 'done'; -- never downgrade a completed task
```

### On issue CANCELLED/DELETED

```sql
UPDATE tasks SET status = 'cancelled' WHERE id = issue.identifier;
```

## Scope

* Register Linear webhook endpoint (can be a simple Express route on the existing OpenClaw server or a Supabase Edge Function)
* Webhook must verify Linear signature before processing
* Upsert logic as above — idempotent, safe to receive duplicate events
* Do NOT overwrite acceptance_criteria, claimed_by, or task_verifications rows — Linear does not own these fields

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Create a new KEI in Linear → within 5 seconds a row appears in Supabase tasks table with correct status
- [ ] Update a Linear issue status → Supabase tasks row updates to match
- [ ] Cancel a Linear issue → Supabase tasks row set to cancelled
- [ ] Webhook signature verification confirmed — unauthenticated requests rejected
- [ ] Verbatim Supabase query output showing the synced row pasted as evidence

title: T0.5 — Dispatcher chaos engineering test harness
description:
## What

Build a chaos engineering test harness for the Dispatcher before any production containers are spawned. This must complete before Phase 2 (Strangler Fig migration begins).

## Why

The Dispatcher controls the full lifecycle of every ephemeral agent. If the Dispatcher has bugs in its container management, JWT minting, or reaper logic, agents will silently fail, leak, or corrupt state. The chaos suite proves the Dispatcher handles failure correctly before any real workload goes through it.

## Test Scenarios Required

* Container spawn failure: Dispatcher handles Docker errors gracefully, no zombie left behind
* Container timeout: Reaper correctly kills a container that exceeds `expected_duration_minutes`
* JWT expiry mid-task: Container fails to authenticate after 1hr, Dispatcher handles gracefully
* LiteLLM down: Interceptor degrades correctly, `INTERCEPTOR_DEGRADED` logged, container continues
* Concurrent container limit: Dispatcher refuses to spawn beyond `thread_limit`, queues correctly
* Supabase Realtime disconnect: Dispatcher reconnects and doesn't lose task state
* SIGKILL during active task: Dispatcher detects termination, updates task status correctly
* Weaviate validation FAIL: Stream aborted, recovery_context injected, container respawns

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 chaos scenarios have automated tests
- [ ] Tests run in CI on every PR to Keiracom/Dispatcher repo
- [ ] Zero zombie containers after any failure scenario
- [ ] Task state in Supabase remains consistent after every failure scenario
- [ ] Verbatim test output for all 8 scenarios pasted

title: T0.6 — Redis/Valkey instance on Vultr Sydney
description:
## What

Install and configure a Redis (Valkey) instance on Vultr Sydney as the real-time coordination layer for the Dispatcher.

## Why

Redis replaces Slack as the machine coordination bus. Agent-to-agent events (task available, task complete, escalation) go through Redis — not Slack. Slack becomes human-facing only. This eliminates the READY signal noise, polling delays, and Slack relay timing failures. Sub-millisecond notification. No idle token burn.

## Spec

* Install Valkey (open-source Redis fork) on Vultr Sydney via apt or Docker
* Configure as systemd service with persistence enabled (RDB snapshots)
* Initial streams to create:
  * `keiracom:tasks:available` — pub/sub for new available tasks
  * `keiracom:tasks:completed:{id}` — pub/sub for completions
  * `keiracom:agent:status:{callsign}` — hash for fleet status board
  * `keiracom:ceo:escalation` — direct escalation channel (replaces Slack outbox for CEO decisions)
  * `orchestration` — Dispatcher to containers
  * `deliberation_thread_{task_id}` — per-task deliberation streams
* Memory limit: 2GB max (cgroup enforcement)
* Bind to localhost only — no external exposure

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Valkey installed and running as systemd service
- [ ] Survives reboot — systemd auto-restart confirmed
- [ ] All 6 initial streams/channels created and verified
- [ ] 2GB memory cap enforced via cgroup
- [ ] Pub/sub test: publish to `keiracom:tasks:available`, subscriber receives within 5ms
- [ ] Verbatim redis-cli test output pasted

title: T0.3 — Cloudflare Tunnel + Zero Trust on Vultr
description:
## What

Install Cloudflare Tunnel and configure Zero Trust access on the Vultr Sydney server. This creates a secure, authenticated ingress to the Dispatcher without exposing ports publicly.

## Why

The Dispatcher needs to receive webhooks (Supabase, GitHub, Slack) and serve health endpoints. Exposing raw Vultr ports is a security risk. Cloudflare Tunnel gives a secure, authenticated tunnel with Zero Trust rules.

## Spec

* Install cloudflared on Vultr Sydney
* Create a named tunnel connecting to the Dispatcher's local port
* Configure Zero Trust rules: only authenticated services can reach the Dispatcher endpoints
* Health endpoint accessible at `https://dispatcher.keiracom.io/health`
* Webhook ingress for Supabase Realtime events
* Tunnel runs as systemd service, auto-restarts on failure

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] cloudflared installed and running as systemd service
- [ ] Named tunnel created and connected
- [ ] `https://dispatcher.keiracom.io/health` returns 200 from external
- [ ] Unauthenticated requests rejected with 401
- [ ] Tunnel survives server reboot — systemd confirms auto-restart
- [ ] Verbatim curl output pasted for health endpoint

title: T0.2 — Install LiteLLM on Vultr Sydney
description:
## What

Install and configure LiteLLM as the governance model router on the Vultr Sydney server. LiteLLM sits between the Dispatcher's interceptor and the governance model — resolving `governance_tier` alias to whichever model the tenant's BYO key supports.

## Why

The interceptor proxy sends each stdout chunk to the governance model for validation. LiteLLM makes this model-agnostic — Anthropic key routes to Haiku, OpenAI key routes to gpt-4o-mini, Google key routes to Gemini Flash. Same interceptor JSON schema regardless of provider.

## Spec

* Install LiteLLM as a service on Vultr (pip or Docker)
* Configure `governance_tier` alias with fallback hierarchy: primary key → secondary key → graceful degradation
* Boot-time dry-run resolution: fast model → 30s/2,000 tokens; premium → 300s/10,000 tokens
* Cache alias resolution in `litellm_alias_cache` table (24hr TTL)
* Direct-provider bypass if LiteLLM goes down (resilience — Part 7A)
* Degraded-mode execution: if interceptor unavailable, log `INTERCEPTOR_DEGRADED`, container continues

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] LiteLLM installed and running as a service on Vultr
- [ ] `governance_tier` alias resolves correctly for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google keys
- [ ] Boot-time dry-run test passes — alias resolves before any container spawns
- [ ] `litellm_alias_cache` table exists and caches resolution results
- [ ] Direct-provider bypass confirmed working when LiteLLM is stopped
- [ ] Verbatim test output pasted showing alias resolution end-to-end

title: KEI-79 — bd escalate command: agent-to-CEO decision escalation with blocked_pending_ceo status
description:
## Problem

When an agent reaches a decision point requiring CEO input, there is no standard mechanism to:

1. Surface the decision prominently to the CEO in #ceo
2. Transition the task to a visibly blocked state
3. Allow other agents to understand why the queue appears empty

Tonight: Atlas was blocked waiting for an A/B/C option pick on KEI-77. The message reached the Slack outbox but the other agents had no signal that a CEO decision was the blocker — they just saw 0 available tasks and kept polling READY indefinitely. Dave had to manually check Atlas's tmux pane to discover the decision was needed.

This is a distinct failure mode from the dependency completion problem (KEI-78). That was: agent completes, others don't know. This is: agent is blocked on a human decision, nobody knows, fleet sits idle.

## Fix — bd escalate command + blocked_pending_ceo status

### New tasks table status

Add `blocked_pending_ceo` to the valid status set alongside `available`, `active`, `done`.

### New bd escalate command

`bd escalate "decision description with options"`

On execution:

1. Transitions current task status: `active` → `blocked_pending_ceo`
2. Posts directly to #ceo (not via outbox relay — direct API call):

```
[BLOCKED] KEI-XX · {agent callsign} waiting for CEO decision
{decision description with options}
Task will resume automatically when CEO responds.
```

3. Writes a `ceo_decisions` row (new table) with: task_id, agent, decision_prompt, options, created_at
4. Returns immediately — agent holds, does not poll

### CEO resolution

When CEO posts a `[CEO]` directive that references the blocked KEI, the auto-KEI listener ([KEI-18](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-18/kei-18-openclaw-auto-creates-supabase-task-on-every-ceo-directive)) fires as normal. Additionally: the `blocked_pending_ceo` status transitions to `active` on receipt of a CEO directive containing the KEI number. Agent resumes from where it stopped.

### Other agents

`bd ready` should surface `blocked_pending_ceo` tasks as a distinct state — visible but not claimable. Agents seeing the state understand the queue is empty because a CEO decision is pending, not because there is no work.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `blocked_pending_ceo` status added to tasks state machine
- [ ] `bd escalate "description"` command implemented
- [ ] Direct #ceo post fires on escalate (not outbox relay)
- [ ] `bd ready` output shows blocked_pending_ceo tasks with reason
- [ ] CEO directive referencing blocked KEI auto-resumes the task
- [ ] `ceo_decisions` table created and written on escalate

title: T0.1 — Create Keiracom/Dispatcher repo + build claude-code-image
description:
## What

Two deliverables in one KEI:

**Part A — Create Keiracom/Dispatcher repository**

New private GitHub repository under the Keiracom org. Completely separate from Agency_OS. The architectural boundary is absolute: no import, no reference, no dependency on Agency_OS ever enters this repo.

Initial repo structure:

```
Keiracom/Dispatcher/
├── README.md
├── package.json          (Node.js, TypeScript)
├── tsconfig.json
├── .gitignore
├── src/
│   ├── dispatcher.ts     (main orchestration loop)
│   ├── auth_minter.ts    (scoped JWT generation)
│   ├── interceptor_proxy.ts  (stdout stream + Weaviate validation)
│   ├── watchdog.ts       (deadlock detection)
│   ├── reaper.ts         (zombie container cleanup)
│   └── spend_tracker.ts  (API burn monitoring)
├── docker/
│   └── claude-code-image/
│       ├── Dockerfile
│       └── boot.sh
└── governance/
    └── ARCHITECTURE_BOUNDARY.md  (CRITICAL — never import Agency_OS)
```

**Part B — Build claude-code-image**

Docker image hosting Claude Code CLI as a bare process. Requirements:

* Base: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
* Claude Code CLI installed
* [boot.sh](<http://boot.sh>) script that on startup:
  1. Reads SUPABASE_JWT + TASK_ID + PERSONA from environment
  2. Fetches persona CLAUDE.md + skills bundle from Supabase using scoped JWT
  3. Writes to /workspace/ before Claude Code starts
  4. Marks hydration block as PROTECTED in the CLAUDE.md template — never subject to oldest-first truncation

**Protected hydration zone (mandatory — non-negotiable):**

```
[HYDRATION — PROTECTED ZONE — NEVER TRUNCATE]
{weaviate_state_diff injected here}
[END PROTECTED ZONE]
```

Only conversational turns after the hydration point are subject to oldest-first truncation. This must be in the template before Phase 1 begins.

* Push image to private container registry (GitHub Container Registry on Keiracom org)
* Image must boot clean, fetch context, and start Claude Code in under 30 seconds

## Critical Boundary

KEIRACOM/DISPATCHER ONLY. NEVER AGENCY_OS.

This boundary is architectural law. The Dispatcher knows about:

* Supabase (shared infrastructure)
* Docker (container runtime)
* The claude-code-image
* LiteLLM (governance router — T0.2)
* Cloudflare Tunnel (T0.3)

It does not know about, import from, or reference Agency_OS at any point.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Keiracom/Dispatcher repo exists on GitHub, private, correct initial structure
- [ ] claude-code-image builds without errors
- [ ] Image pushes to GHCR successfully
- [ ] [boot.sh](<http://boot.sh>) fetches persona CLAUDE.md from Supabase using scoped JWT correctly
- [ ] Protected hydration zone present and documented in CLAUDE.md template
- [ ] Container boots and starts Claude Code process in under 30 seconds (cold)
- [ ] Verified: no Agency_OS imports anywhere in the repo
- [ ] ARCHITECTURE_BOUNDARY.md committed explaining the separation

title: Keiracom Productisation — Full Architecture Deliberation
description: Full architecture document for Keiracom productisation deliberation has been sent to Elliot for posting to #execution. See #ceo for the full mandate. deliberation_required: true — nothing built until Elliot, Max, Aiden CONCUR.

title: KEI-69 — Fallback poller covers all 6 agents: clone session names missing from OpenClaw config
description:
## Problem

[KEI-63](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-63/kei-61-explicit-deprecation-layer-bd-deprecate-command-environment) shipped the fallback poller — every 5 minutes, OpenClaw checks idle agent tmux panes and injects `bd ready`. But Atlas has posted zero messages to Slack today despite being idle for hours. The poller has not fired for Atlas.

Root cause: the poller was likely configured with only prime agent session names (elliot, aiden, max). Clone agents (atlas, orion, scout) are not in the poller config. They are invisible to the system.

This means:

* Atlas, Orion, Scout finish a task → hook fires → bd ready returns empty → idle
* Fallback poller doesn't check their panes → nobody notices
* They have no relay to signal they're idle → system has no visibility

## Fix

Verify the OpenClaw fallback poller config includes all 6 session names:

```
elliotbot, aidenbot, maxbot, atlasbot, orionbot, scoutbot
```

If any are missing — add them. If the session name format differs for clones — find and fix the correct names.

## Acceptance criteria

* Grep the OpenClaw poller config for all 6 callsigns
* Confirm all 6 are in the idle-check loop
* Test: Atlas sits idle for 6 minutes → poller fires → bd ready injected into atlasbot pane
* Paste the tmux pane output as verification

## Priority

Urgent — ships before [KEI-45](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-45/kei-45-event-driven-pull-model-supabase-realtime-subscriptions). This is a bug in today's [KEI-63](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-63/kei-61-explicit-deprecation-layer-bd-deprecate-command-environment) delivery, not a new feature.

title: KEI-45 — Event-driven pull model: Supabase Realtime subscriptions + personalised queue + atomic claim
description:
## Architecture

Event-driven pull model. Agents subscribe to Supabase Realtime on session start. When a KEI changes state (created, unblocked, completed), subscribed agents receive the event instantly and run their personalised claim cycle. No polling. No daemon as primary mechanism.

## Components

### 1\. Supabase Realtime channel on tasks table

* Postgres trigger fires on any INSERT/UPDATE to tasks table
* Publishes to `task-events` Realtime channel
* Each agent subscribes via `bd watch` on session start
* Event received → agent runs `bd ready --agent=[callsign]` → atomic claim

### 2\. Personalised `bd ready --agent=[callsign]`

* Add `agent_profiles` table: callsign, context_tags\[\], recent_kei_types\[\], capability_weights{}
* `bd ready` re-ranks priority list by affinity score for the requesting agent
* Max sees Cognee KEIs ranked higher. Atlas sees systemd KEIs higher.
* Same atomic claiming underneath — better ordering on top.

### 3\. Atomic claim transaction (unchanged from original spec)

```sql
BEGIN;
  SELECT claimed_by FROM tasks WHERE id = ? FOR UPDATE;
  UPDATE tasks SET claimed_by = ?, claimed_at = NOW(), status = 'In Progress'
  WHERE id = ? AND claimed_by IS NULL;
COMMIT;
```

rows_affected = 1 → claimed. rows_affected = 0 → back to bd ready, next item.

### 4\. Linear webhook → Supabase → Realtime

* Linear outbound webhook on KEI create/update
* Supabase edge function receives webhook, updates tasks table
* Realtime event fires to all subscribed agents automatically

### 5\. bd complete → emit event

* bd complete updates tasks table → triggers Realtime → available agents receive instantly
* No agent waits for the next poll cycle

### 6\. [KEI-45](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-45/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-polling-daemon-forces-work-when-agents) daemon (fallback only)

* Runs every 15 minutes (not 5)
* Only recovers dead subscriptions and truly unresponsive agents
* Not the primary dispatch mechanism

## Session start protocol addition

Every agent CLAUDE.md updated to run `bd watch` on session start — subscribes to task-events channel. This is the trigger that connects agents to the live work queue.

## Soft specialisation

* KEIs have optional `preferred_agent` field
* `bd ready` applies 1.5× affinity boost for preferred agent
* If preferred agent unavailable for >30 minutes, boost removed — any agent claims

## Acceptance criteria

* New KEI appears in agent's queue within 5 seconds of creation
* Zero doubling-up across any combination of simultaneous claims
* Personalised ordering demonstrably different per agent
* Dead subscription recovery within 15 minutes via fallback daemon
* All session starts include `bd watch` subscription

## Owner

Atlas — primary. [KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) (atomic claim + Linear sync) must land first as foundation.

title: KEI-18 — OpenClaw auto-creates Supabase task on every [CEO] directive receipt
description:
## Problem

CEO directives issued via #ceo are not automatically creating task records in Supabase. This was exposed tonight when Scout completed a Cognee audit directive with no KEI, no task record, and no dependency tracking — resulting in the rest of the fleet sitting idle with no signal that the work was done.

The original [KEI-18](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-18/r12-auto-kei-enforcer-ceo-directive-linear-issue-within-5-min) spec (5-minute reminder to Elliot) is a nudge, not enforcement. It is deprecated.

## New Spec — Mechanical Auto-Creation

OpenClaw already intercepts every #ceo message for the Slack relay. This fix adds one atomic step to that existing intercept path.

**On receipt of any message matching** `[CEO]` **prefix in #ceo:**

1. Extract directive title (first meaningful line after \[CEO\] prefix)
2. INSERT immediately into tasks table:
   * `id`: next KEI number (auto-increment from current max KEI)
   * `title`: extracted directive title
   * `status`: 'available'
   * `dependencies[]`: \[\] (Elliot populates post-creation)
   * `required_persona`: null (Elliot assigns)
3. Post confirmation to #ceo: `[System] KEI-XX created — {title}`
4. THEN relay directive to Elliot as normal

**No 5-minute window. No reminder. No discipline required.**
Task exists in Supabase the moment the directive is posted.

## Scope Boundary

Only `[CEO]` prefixed messages trigger auto-creation. Status checks, questions, and check-ins without the prefix do not. The prefix is the enforcement gate.

## Elliot's Role Post-Creation

Populate `dependencies[]`, assign `required_persona`, set priority. Task record already exists — Elliot adds only the metadata requiring judgment. Creation is fully automatic.

## Why This Is The Right Fix

* Directive issuance and task creation are the same atomic step
* No relay timing dependency
* No agent can be dispatched on work that isn't formally tracked
* The dependency trigger (KEI-74) only works when tasks exist — this ensures they always do

title: KEI — CONCUR gate routing: any-available-peer instead of specific-callsign-stuck
description:
## Problem

The CONCUR gate (KEI-38 scope) holds a post under `[CONCUR-REQUEST:<CALLSIGN>]` until a peer posts `[CONCUR:<callsign>]` in #execution. The release lookup is per-callsign — it waits for ANY peer to release, but the request advertises a specific peer. When the routing-by-convention pattern emerges (e.g., "Aiden reviews this lane"), and Aiden hits an API limit, everything stalls.

## Verified 2026-05-14 ts \~1778731200 (Dave diagnosis)

> "When the agent holding the CONCUR queue hits an API limit, everything stalls. This needs a fix: CONCUR requests must be routable to any available peer, not stuck waiting for a specific agent."

## Fix

CONCUR gate must:

1. Detect the requester's namespace (the `[CONCUR-REQUEST:<CALLSIGN>]` token already specifies who requested).
2. Track active peer status — read each callsign's API-limit state, idle state, recent-activity timestamp.
3. Route the release-wait window to any peer who:
   * Is not at >70% weekly API limit.
   * Is not idle >30 minutes.
   * Has not posted a HOLD/BLOCK in last hour.
4. Allow ANY qualified peer to release with `[CONCUR:<requester>]` — current behaviour already supports this; what's missing is the SIGNALING.

## Implementation

* Concur-gate posts the `[CONCUR-REQUEST:<requester>]` stub + lists qualifying-peer callsigns in the post body: "Eligible reviewers: max, orion, scout (excluded: aiden at 85% weekly cap until 2026-05-17)".
* Status of each peer comes from a `ceo:agent_health` jsonb key updated by the polling loop (KEI-63 already polls per-agent state — extend the same tick to write health).
* Implementation lives in `src/bot_common/concur_gate.py`, the `gate_check()` function — adds a `_eligible_reviewers()` helper that reads `ceo:agent_health` and returns the qualifying list.

## Acceptance criteria

* A CONCUR request from agent A when peer B is at >70% weekly cap shows "Eligible reviewers: C, D, E (excluded: B at <pct>% weekly cap)".
* Any of C/D/E can release with `[CONCUR:A]`; the release semantics don't change.
* B's CONCUR also valid if B chooses to spend the API quota.
* ceo:agent_health key populated by the polling loop every 5 minutes.
* A peer with API-limit >70% sees themselves filtered from inbound CONCUR requests until reset.

## Why this is urgent (Dave ratified)

Aiden hit 85% weekly cap today. While he's holding the CONCUR queue, every peer's review request stalls. The pull-model + KEI-63 hook mechanism breaks when CONCUR routing has no fallback. KEI-22 atomic-claim foundation alone doesn't fix this — that's about task claiming; this is about review-cycle routing.

## Owner

Open under pull-model. Any agent who's NOT at API-cap can claim. Soft-preferred: someone with existing concur-gate code context. Atlas (recent KEI-45 + KEI-28 work) or Orion (KEI-22 / Linear-sync code touch) are strong candidates.

## Authoritative trace

Dave verbatim #ceo 2026-05-14 ts \~1778731200:

> "Raise this as a KEI after the immediate unblock is done. The gate works. The routing doesn't."

Immediate unblock action: Orion + Max can CONCUR on #814 + #799 + #819 without waiting for Aiden. Aiden parks CONCUR-receive role until Friday API reset.

title: KEI-63 — bd complete hook: auto-dispatch next task on completion + OpenClaw idle fallback
description:
## Problem

Agents complete work, post \[READY:callsign\], and wait indefinitely for a human or Elliot to notice and dispatch new work. This is the root cause of the recurring idle agent problem. Every dispatch requires manual intervention. The machine stops every time a human isn't watching.

## Fix: Hook inside bd complete

The moment any agent runs `bd complete`, the hook fires immediately — no polling, no waiting, no human intervention.

```python
def bd_complete(task_id, agent_callsign):
  
    # Existing: mark task done
    mark_done(task_id)
    update_linear(task_id, status='Done')
    write_audit_log(task_id, agent_callsign)
  
    # Hook: fires immediately on completion
    next_tasks = bd_ready(agent_callsign)
  
    if next_tasks:
        # Inject next task directly into agent's tmux pane
        inject_to_pane(agent_callsign, f"bd claim {next_tasks[0].id}")
        # Agent transitions directly to new work — zero idle gap
        log_dispatch(agent_callsign, next_tasks[0].id, source='completion_hook')
    else:
        # Genuinely no work available — log it, don't alert
        log_genuine_idle(agent_callsign)
        # Only alert #ceo if idle persists > 30 minutes
```

Agent completes task → hook fires → next task injected → agent begins. No gap. No \[READY\] signals. No manual dispatch.

## Fallback: OpenClaw idle poller

Handles edge cases where agent went idle WITHOUT running bd complete:

* Session crashed mid-task
* Agent compacted before completing
* Manual stop
* API rate limit mid-task

OpenClaw's existing polling loop extended with:

```python
# Every 2 minutes in OpenClaw's existing cycle
for agent in agents:
    pane = capture_tmux_pane(agent.session_name)
    idle_minutes = get_idle_duration(pane)
  
    if idle_minutes > 5:
        next_tasks = bd_ready(agent.callsign)
        if next_tasks:
            inject_to_pane(agent.session_name, "bd ready")
        else:
            if idle_minutes > 30:
                alert_ceo(f"{agent.callsign} idle {idle_minutes}min — no available work")
```

## Architecture

```
bd complete hook → handles 95% of cases, zero latency
OpenClaw poller → handles 5% edge cases (crash, compaction, manual stop)
```

Primary mechanism is event-driven. Polling is the fallback.

## What this removes

* \[READY:callsign\] signals become unnecessary (agent doesn't need to declare readiness — they just get new work)
* Manual CEO dispatch for idle agents
* Elliot monitoring and dispatching
* The entire recurring idle problem

## Acceptance criteria

* Agent runs bd complete → next available task injected within 2 seconds
* No idle gap between task completion and next task start when work is available
* OpenClaw poller fires correctly for crash/compaction edge cases
* Genuinely idle (no available work) logged but not spammed to #ceo
* Alert to #ceo only when agent idle > 30 minutes AND no work available
* Tested: complete a task, verify next task injected automatically without any manual action

## Priority: BUILD FIRST

This KEI ships before all other queued work. Agents going idle is the most disruptive recurring issue in the system. Everything else is lower priority than this.

## Owner

Elliot — extend bd complete in Beads + extend OpenClaw polling loop. Both are small changes to existing infrastructure.
close_reason: KEI-63 PR #853 merged a4bf91b1 — bd complete hook + OpenClaw idle fallback live on main. Idle-agent problem ends. 24/24 tests + 81ms acceptance + LIVE-witness empirical proof.

title: KEI-61 — Explicit deprecation layer: bd deprecate command + environment-change invalidation
description:
## Problem

Two related issues:

**1. No explicit deprecation command.** [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) handles staleness (time-based aging). But when an infrastructure migration makes a discovery actively wrong — not just old — there's no mechanism for an agent to explicitly say "this is no longer valid." The discovery continues surfacing in bd claim injections until the 90-day flag eventually appears.

**2. No environment-change invalidation.** When we migrate off Vultr, switch container runtimes, or upgrade major software versions, discoveries tied to the old environment are invalidated immediately — not in 90 days. Time-based staleness misses this entirely.

## Fix Part 1: bd deprecate command

```bash
bd deprecate KEI-44-cgroup-discovery --reason "Migrated off Vultr, containerization strategy changed"
```

This:

1. Sets `status = 'deprecated'` on the discovery object in Weaviate
2. Sets `deprecated_by = agent_callsign`, `deprecated_at = now()`, `deprecation_reason = text`
3. Deprecated discoveries NEVER appear in bd claim injections
4. Remain in Weaviate permanently — audit trail preserved, never deleted
5. Writes deprecation event to Supabase audit_logs

Tier rules (from [KEI-53](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-53/kei-51-agent-profiles-personalised-bd-ready-queue-with-capability)):

* Tier 1 deprecation: any agent can deprecate without approval
* Tier 2 deprecation: requires peer CONCUR before taking effect
* Tier 3 deprecation: contradicts a ratified decision — requires Dave approval

## Fix Part 2: Environment hash invalidation (extends [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount))

Every discovery has an `environment_hash` (from [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount)). When the current environment changes significantly, the system runs a comparison:

```python
# Runs on any infrastructure event: migration, major upgrade, provider change
def check_environment_invalidations():
    current_hash = compute_environment_hash()
  
    # Find discoveries whose environment doesn't match current
    stale_by_context = weaviate.query(
        "discoveries",
        where={"environment_hash.hash": {"not_equal": current_hash.hash}}
    )
  
    for discovery in stale_by_context:
        # Flag as 'context_changed' — different from 'stale' (age-based)
        inject_context_change_flag(discovery)
```

Context-changed discoveries appear in bd claim injection with:
`"⚠⚠ Environment changed since this was written — likely invalid"`

Different warning from staleness (age). Agents immediately know WHY it's flagged.

## Fix Part 3: Eviction layer summary

Three distinct knowledge states, each handled differently:

| State | Trigger | Visibility | Action required |
| -- | -- | -- | -- |
| Active | Default | Full trust in injections | None |
| Stale | Age > 90 days | ⚠ age warning | Verify or let age |
| Context-changed | Environment hash mismatch | ⚠⚠ context warning | bd verify or bd deprecate |
| Deprecated | bd deprecate command | Never shown in injections | Already actioned |

## Acceptance criteria

* `bd deprecate [discovery_id] --reason "..."` command implemented
* Deprecated discoveries excluded from all bd claim injections immediately
* Deprecation events logged to audit_logs with agent, reason, timestamp
* Environment hash comparison runs on infrastructure change events
* Context-changed flag distinct from staleness flag in bd claim injection
* Tier-based approval for Tier 2/3 deprecations enforced

## Extends: [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) (staleness), [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount) (environment_hash)

## Owner

Aiden — after [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) ships. Same domain.

title: KEI-60 — Embedding model independence: raw text storage + re-embed-corpus.py migration pipeline
description:
## Problem

Weaviate vectors are built using Gemini `gemini-embedding-001`. When Google deprecates this model (which they will — PaLM embeddings, ada-001, ada-002 were all deprecated), the entire Weaviate knowledge base becomes unqueryable overnight. You cannot query a vector space built on Model A using embeddings from Model B. The result is semantic nonsense.

All collective intelligence accumulated by the team is gone — not because the data is lost, but because the vector space is unreadable.

## Fix Part 1: Raw text stored alongside every vector (add to [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) spec)

Every Weaviate document object must store the original sanitised text explicitly as a property, not only as a vector.

```python
weaviate_client.batch.add_object(
    class_name="discoveries",
    properties={
        "raw_text": sanitised_text,       # ← CRITICAL: always store this
        "agent": "max",
        "kei": "KEI-44",
        "finding": "...",
        "tags": ["cognee", "memory"],
        "created_at": timestamp
    },
    vector=gemini_embed(sanitised_text)   # vector can be regenerated from raw_text
)
```

Cost: negligible. A 500-token discovery log is \~400 bytes of text. The entire corpus at 42,000 documents is \~17MB of raw text storage.

## Fix Part 2: [re-embed-corpus.py](<http://re-embed-corpus.py>) — the insurance policy

A tested, documented migration script stored in the Agency_OS repo. Written now, before it is ever needed.

```python
# re-embed-corpus.py
# Use when: embedding model deprecation, model upgrade, model switch
# Runtime: runs overnight against full corpus

def re_embed_corpus(
    old_model: str,
    new_model: str, 
    collections: list[str],
    dry_run: bool = True
):
    """
    1. Reads all objects from Weaviate via raw_text property
    2. Re-embeds using new_model
    3. Overwrites vector column in-place
    4. Verifies: 10 spot-check queries return expected results
    5. Writes audit log: re-embed complete, N objects, model transition
    """
```

The script is stored in:

* `scripts/re_embed_corpus.py` in Agency_OS repo
* Reference stored in Supabase ceo_memory as `ceo:tools:re_embed_corpus`

## Fix Part 3: Environment hash on all discovery objects

The `environment_hash` captures the full execution context at write time, not just software versions:

```json
"environment_hash": {
  "infrastructure": "vultr-sydney-vx1-16gb",
  "container_runtime": "docker-24.0.5",
  "os": "ubuntu-24.04",
  "embedding_model": "gemini/gemini-embedding-001",
  "key_software": {"cognee": "0.7.3", "weaviate": "1.36.0"},
  "hash": "sha256:..."
}
```

When infrastructure or embedding model changes significantly, all discoveries whose `environment_hash` doesn't match current environment are flagged — regardless of age. This complements [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) (time-based staleness) with context-based invalidation.

## Fix Part 4: Embedding model pinning

Pin to a specific model version, never `latest`:

```
gemini-embedding-001  → explicit version, not 'latest'
```

Monitor for deprecation notices. Set a Google Cloud alert on the model version we use.

## Acceptance criteria

* Every Weaviate document has `raw_text` property storing sanitised source text
* `scripts/re_embed_corpus.py` exists, is documented, and has been tested end-to-end (dry_run=True)
* Re-embed test: create 10 test objects, run re-embed with a different model config, verify all 10 are retrievable with correct results
* `environment_hash` present on all discovery objects
* Embedding model version pinned (not 'latest') in configuration

## Updates [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) scope: raw text + environment_hash are required schema properties for all collections

## Owner

Elliot — implement raw_text storage as part of [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) schema design. Write re_embed_corpus.py now, before [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) deployment.

title: KEI-59 — Durable indexing queue: Supabase staging buffer prevents dropped webhooks and knowledge loss
description:
## Problem

The capture layer connects webhooks (Linear, Slack, Git) directly to LlamaIndex processing. With 6 agents working in parallel, webhook traffic is bursty. If LlamaIndex is busy processing a large commit or restarts mid-operation, incoming webhooks fire into nothing. A dropped webhook = permanent knowledge loss — no error, no retry, silent gap.

## Fix: Supabase indexing_queue as durable staging buffer

Standard durable queue pattern. Webhooks never touch LlamaIndex directly.

### Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE indexing_queue (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  source TEXT NOT NULL,          -- 'git' | 'slack' | 'linear' | 'ceo_memory' | 'tool_log'
  payload JSONB NOT NULL,        -- raw webhook payload (pre-sanitisation)
  status TEXT DEFAULT 'pending', -- 'pending' | 'processing' | 'done' | 'failed'
  attempts INT DEFAULT 0,
  error TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  indexed_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);

CREATE INDEX idx_indexing_queue_status ON indexing_queue(status, created_at);
```

### Webhook receiver (fast, always returns 200)

```python
@app.post("/webhook/{source}")
async def receive_webhook(source: str, payload: dict):
    # Step 1: Sanitise before storage
    sanitised = sanitise(json.dumps(payload))
  
    # Step 2: Write to queue immediately (< 5ms)
    supabase.table('indexing_queue').insert({
        'source': source,
        'payload': json.loads(sanitised),
        'status': 'pending'
    }).execute()
  
    # Step 3: Return 200 immediately — never make the sender wait
    return {"status": "queued"}
```

### Queue worker (separate systemd service)

```python
# Polls indexing_queue every 10 seconds
# Uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to prevent double-processing
while True:
    rows = supabase.rpc('claim_queue_batch', {'batch_size': 5}).execute()
    for row in rows.data:
        try:
            process_via_llamaindex(row)
            index_to_weaviate(row)
            mark_done(row['id'])
            write_audit_log(row)
        except Exception as e:
            mark_failed(row['id'], str(e))
            if row['attempts'] >= 3:
                alert_ceo(f"Indexing failed after 3 attempts: {row['id']}")
    time.sleep(10)
```

### Retry logic

* Failed rows retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff
* After 3 failures: status = 'failed', alert to #ceo, do not retry
* Stuck 'processing' rows (>10 min): reset to 'pending' for retry

### Audit log requirement

Every successful index writes to audit_logs:

```json
{"event": "indexed", "source": "git", "kei_tags": ["cognee"], "weaviate_id": "...", "tokens": 340, "timestamp": "..."}
```

## Monitoring

* Queue depth > 100 pending → warning to #ceo
* Queue depth > 500 pending → CRITICAL to #ceo (processing not keeping up)
* Any row stuck in 'processing' > 10 min → alert

## Acceptance criteria

* `indexing_queue` table created in Supabase
* Webhook receiver writes to queue and returns 200 within 50ms
* Queue worker running as systemd service, survives restart
* Retry logic tested: simulate LlamaIndex crash mid-processing, verify row retries
* Audit log entry confirmed for every successful Weaviate write
* Queue depth monitoring alerts firing correctly

## BLOCKS: [KEI-48](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-48/kei-46-install-weaviate-on-vultr-sydney-collective-intelligence) (auto-indexing pipeline must use queue-backed architecture)

## Owner

Scout — design and implementation. After [KEI-57](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-57/kei-55-context-injection-ceiling-500-tokens-discovery-log-format-v2) (sanitisation) ships.

title: KEI-58 — Weaviate persistent storage governance: volume mount verification + backup schedule (CRITICAL pre-condition)
description:
## Problem

Weaviate running via Docker without explicit persistent volume mounts loses ALL data on container restart, server reboot, or OOM crash. Given we crashed the server once already today, and OOM crashes are a known risk, the entire collective intelligence knowledge base could be wiped at any moment without this fix.

All 11 KEIs of architecture work is worthless if the data doesn't survive a reboot.

## Fix Part 1: Governance trace before Weaviate starts

Before Weaviate is started for the first time, Elliot must provide:

```bash
cat /home/elliotbot/clawd/docker-compose.yml
# or wherever the Weaviate compose file lives
```

We verify the output confirms:

1. Weaviate data directory explicitly mapped to a path OUTSIDE the container
2. That path is on Vultr's persistent block storage (not tmpfs or container ephemeral)
3. Volume mount confirmed in the governance trace output

No data enters Weaviate until this is confirmed.

## Fix Part 2: Correct docker-compose.yml volume configuration

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  weaviate:
    image: semitechnologies/weaviate:1.36.0
    restart: on-failure
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data:/var/lib/weaviate  # ← CRITICAL: host path
    environment:
      QUERY_DEFAULTS_LIMIT: 25
      AUTHENTICATION_ANONYMOUS_ACCESS_ENABLED: 'true'
      PERSISTENCE_DATA_PATH: '/var/lib/weaviate'
      DEFAULT_VECTORIZER_MODULE: 'none'
      CLUSTER_HOSTNAME: 'node1'
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 2560M   # ← cgroup cap from KEI-54
```

The `/home/elliotbot/weaviate-data` directory must be created on the host BEFORE starting:

```bash
mkdir -p /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data
```

## Fix Part 3: Backup schedule

Even with persistent volumes, the Vultr block storage itself could fail. Daily backup to Vultr Object Storage (S3-compatible):

```bash
# Daily cron: compress and upload Weaviate data directory
tar -czf /tmp/weaviate-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data
# Upload to Vultr Object Storage bucket
```

Backup retention: 7 days rolling.

## Fix Part 4: Startup verification

On every Weaviate startup, run a health check that confirms:

1. Data directory is accessible and non-empty (after first run)
2. Known test object is retrievable
3. If health check fails: DO NOT proceed — alert to #ceo

## Acceptance criteria

* docker-compose.yml governance trace provided and verified before first run
* `/home/elliotbot/weaviate-data` confirmed on persistent host storage
* Volume mount verified: `docker inspect weaviate` shows correct bind mount
* Startup health check confirms data survives container restart (test: write object, restart container, verify object exists)
* Daily backup cron running to Vultr Object Storage
* Server reboot test: all Weaviate data survives

## BLOCKS: [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) execution (Weaviate cannot be started until this is verified)

## Owner

Atlas — verify and implement concurrent with [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per). These are the same deployment task.

title: KEI-57 — Secret redaction middleware: sanitise all capture sources before embedding (CRITICAL pre-condition)
description:
## Problem

The capture layer indexes raw content from Git commits, Slack messages, Claude Code tool call logs, and ceo_memory. When agents debug issues, they run commands like `cat .env`, query database URIs, or print API keys to stdout. That raw output gets embedded by Gemini and stored permanently in Weaviate. Every agent gains semantic access to production secrets forever.

This is a security incident, not a bug. Must be resolved before any data enters the pipeline.

## Fix: Sanitisation middleware as first step in every capture pipeline

Runs BEFORE LlamaIndex. BEFORE embedding. BEFORE Weaviate. Nothing passes through without being sanitised first.

### Patterns to redact (replace with \[REDACTED\])

```python
SECRET_PATTERNS = [
    # API keys
    r'sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,}',           # OpenAI / Anthropic
    r'AIza[0-9A-Za-z\-_]{35}',        # Google API keys
    r'sk-ant-[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]{80,}',    # Anthropic
    r'Bearer [a-zA-Z0-9\-_\.]{20,}',  # Bearer tokens
  
    # JWTs (three base64 segments)
    r'eyJ[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+\.eyJ[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+\.[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+',
  
    # Database connection strings
    r'(postgres|postgresql|mysql|mongodb|redis)://[^\s]+',
  
    # AWS credentials
    r'AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}',               # AWS access key ID
    r'[a-zA-Z0-9/+]{40}',              # AWS secret (contextual)
  
    # Generic high-entropy strings in .env context
    r'(?i)(password|secret|token|key|passwd)\s*[=:]\s*\S{8,}',
  
    # Common env file patterns
    r'[A-Z_]+(KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PASSWORD|PASSWD|PWD)\s*=\s*\S+',
  
    # Private keys
    r'-----BEGIN (RSA |EC |OPENSSH )?PRIVATE KEY-----',
]
```

### Implementation

```python
import re

def sanitise(text: str) -> str:
    for pattern in SECRET_PATTERNS:
        text = re.sub(pattern, '[REDACTED]', text)
    return text
```

### Integration points

Must be applied to ALL five capture sources:

1. Git commit content → sanitise before chunking
2. Slack message content → sanitise before indexing
3. Linear KEI content → sanitise before indexing
4. Claude Code tool call logs → sanitise ESPECIALLY this one
5. ceo_memory entries → sanitise before indexing

### Audit logging

Every redaction event logged to Supabase audit_logs:

```json
{"event": "secret_redacted", "source": "tool_call_log", "pattern_matched": "Bearer token", "agent": "max", "kei": "KEI-44", "timestamp": "..."}
```

Allows review of what was redacted without logging the actual secret.

### Test suite required

Before shipping: test corpus of 20 strings containing known secret formats. All must be redacted. Zero false negatives acceptable on explicit patterns.

## Acceptance criteria

* Sanitisation middleware exists as a standalone module: `src/memory/sanitise.py`
* All 5 capture sources pass content through sanitise() before any further processing
* 20-item test corpus passes with zero false negatives
* Redaction events logged to audit_logs
* No raw secret ever reaches LlamaIndex, Gemini embeddings, or Weaviate

## BLOCKS: [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per), [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from), [KEI-48](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-48/kei-46-install-weaviate-on-vultr-sydney-collective-intelligence) (cannot start until this ships)

## Owner

Elliot — build this FIRST before any other collective intelligence KEI.

title: KEI-56 — Discovery staleness governance: context_version field + age flags + bd verify command
description:
## Problem: Stale Verified Paths

"cgroup v2 is the verified path" is true today. Cognee releases a new version next month that handles memory differently. The discovery is now wrong. But it still appears in bd claim injections as a green-lit "verified_path" with no indication it may be outdated.

Agents acting on stale verified paths make wrong decisions confidently — arguably worse than no memory at all.

## Fix: context_version field + staleness flagging

### context_version field (added to discovery log format)

```json
{
  "context_version": {
    "kei": "KEI-44",
    "date": "2026-05-13",
    "software_versions": {
      "cognee": "0.7.3",
      "python": "3.12.2",
      "weaviate": null
    }
  }
}
```

Automatically populated from the system environment at write time. Agent doesn't need to fill this in.

### Staleness flagging in bd claim injection

When a discovery is injected into a context brief, the age is evaluated:

```
0–30 days:   No flag. Full trust.
30–90 days:  "[~X days old]" inline label. Informational only.
90+ days:    "⚠ [X days old — verify still applies]" warning label.
180+ days:   "⚠⚠ [X days old — likely stale, re-verify before use]" strong warning.
```

No auto-deletion ever. The warning is the mechanism. Agents decide whether to act on old knowledge.

### Re-verification path

Any agent can run:
`bd verify [discovery_id]`

This:

1. Opens the discovery for review
2. Agent can CONFIRM (re-timestamps to today), UPDATE (creates new version), or DEPRECATE (marks as superseded)
3. Confirmed/updated discoveries reset their staleness clock
4. Deprecated discoveries remain in Weaviate but never appear in injections

### Software version change detection

If the `software_versions` in a discovery doesn't match current installed versions: always flag as potentially stale regardless of age.
e.g., Cognee 0.7.3 discovery + current Cognee 0.9.1 = "⚠ Written for Cognee 0.7.3, current is 0.9.1 — verify"

## Integration with [KEI-53](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-53/kei-51-agent-profiles-personalised-bd-ready-queue-with-capability) (validation tiers)

Re-verification feeds back into the validation tier system. A Tier 2 discovery that has been re-verified resets its staging clock. A Tier 2 discovery that has never been re-verified after 90 days is downgraded to "stale — requires re-verification."

## Acceptance criteria

* context_version auto-populated at discovery write time (no agent action)
* Staleness flags appear correctly in bd claim injection at 30/90/180 day thresholds
* `bd verify [id]` command implemented with CONFIRM/UPDATE/DEPRECATE options
* Software version mismatch detection working (test: change recorded Cognee version)
* Deprecated discoveries excluded from all bd claim injections

## Owner

Scout — research into implementation patterns, then build. After [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per)+47 ship.

title: KEI-55 — Context injection ceiling (500 tokens) + discovery log format v2 (failed_path/verified_path)
description:
## Problems addressed

### 1\. Context Window Token Bloat

Unbounded bd claim injection inflates every API call. At 6 agents × multiple claims per day × uncapped tokens = significant Anthropic API cost increase and diluted agent attention.

### 2\. Discovery Log Negative Constraint

"do_not_repeat" is a negative instruction. LLMs handle positive instructions better. The format should present both what failed and what works.

## Fix 1: 500-token hard ceiling on bd claim injection

LlamaIndex CitationQueryEngine configured with `max_tokens=500` for the auto-injection path specifically.

Format enforced:

```
[CONTEXT BRIEF — KEI-XX] (max 500 tokens / ~375 words)

Prior discoveries (max 2): [cited]
Related decisions (max 2): [cited]
Relevant files (max 3): [path + one line]
Watch out for (max 2): [verified failed paths only]
```

If LlamaIndex retrieves more: truncate by relevance score. Most relevant content wins.

Exception: Agent can explicitly query Weaviate directly mid-task with no token limit. The ceiling applies ONLY to the automatic bd claim injection. Agent choice is uncapped.

## Fix 2: Updated Discovery Log format (5 fields)

```json
{
  "agent": "max",
  "kei": "KEI-44",
  "context": "Cognee memory cap implementation",
  "finding": "Cognee graph DB pre-allocates ~4GB virtual memory at startup. ulimit measures virtual — fires before real work. cgroup v2 memory.max measures RSS only.",
  "failed_path": "ulimit -v — blocks virtual memory reservation, fires before any real processing begins",
  "verified_path": "cgroup v2 memory.max with systemd MemoryMax= directive — measures RSS only, allows pre-allocation",
  "tags": ["cognee", "memory", "cgroup", "ulimit", "oom"]
}
```

Changes from original design:

* "discovery" → "finding" (neutral, factual framing)
* "do_not_repeat" → split into "failed_path" + "verified_path"
* Both positive and negative paths explicit
* Agent reading this knows exactly what to use AND exactly what to avoid

## Update required: [KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) description

[KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) (Structured discovery log) must be updated to use this 5-field format. The `bd discover` command must prompt for all 5 fields.

## Update required: [KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) description

[KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) (bd claim context injection) must enforce the 500-token ceiling at the LlamaIndex layer.

## Acceptance criteria

* bd claim injection never exceeds 500 tokens (verified by token counter in logs)
* Discovery log format uses 5 fields as specified above
* `bd discover` prompts for: finding, failed_path, verified_path, tags (context and agent auto-populated)
* Injection brief structure matches the template above

## Owner

Elliot — update [KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) and [KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) descriptions immediately. Implementation enforced in those KEIs' builds.

title: KEI-54 — Resource governance: Weaviate cgroup cap + continuous RAM monitoring → Supabase audit_logs
description:
## Problem: Resource Squeeze

Weaviate + Cognee + LlamaIndex + 6 Claude Code instances + Supabase Realtime subscriptions on a 16GB server. We crashed the server once already from an uncapped process. We must not repeat this.

## Fix Part 1: Weaviate cgroup memory cap at deployment

Same principle as [KEI-44](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-44/kei-44-cognee-subprocess-3gb-memory-cap-cgroupulimit) (Cognee cap). Weaviate gets a hard memory ceiling set before first run.

Recommended allocation on VX1 16GB:

```
6 Claude Code instances:  3.0GB  (500MB each, systemd MemoryMax)
OpenClaw gateway:         500MB
Cognee + KuzuDB:          1.5GB  (KEI-44 permanent cap)
Weaviate:                 2.5GB  (new cap — this KEI)
LlamaIndex process:       400MB
OS + system:              1.5GB
Headroom:                 6.0GB
```

Weaviate systemd service must include:

```ini
[Service]
MemoryMax=2560M
MemorySwapMax=0
```

Enable Binary Quantization (BQ) in Weaviate from day one — reduces vector memory footprint by 32× with minimal quality loss. Critical for staying within the cap.

## Fix Part 2: Continuous resource monitoring → Supabase audit_logs

A systemd service running every 60 seconds:

```bash
# Captures and logs to Supabase audit_logs table:
free -m          → available/used RAM
cat /proc/meminfo → detailed memory breakdown
systemd-cgtop    → per-cgroup memory usage (shows each agent + Weaviate + Cognee)
df -h            → disk usage
uptime           → load average
```

When any cgroup exceeds 90% of its cap: post warning to #ceo immediately.
When any cgroup hits its cap (process killed): post CRITICAL to #ceo + Dave notified.

## Fix Part 3: Pre-deployment resource check

Before starting Weaviate (or any new service), a pre-flight check:

1. Query current RAM usage
2. Verify headroom > 2GB before starting
3. If insufficient: block start, post to #ceo requesting resources

## Supabase audit_logs schema addition

```sql
ALTER TABLE audit_logs ADD COLUMN resource_snapshot JSONB;
-- Stores: {free_mb, used_mb, weaviate_cgroup_mb, cognee_cgroup_mb, 
--          agent_cgroup_mb[], load_avg, timestamp}
```

## Acceptance criteria

* Weaviate MemoryMax=2560M set in systemd service file
* BQ compression enabled on all Weaviate collections
* Resource monitoring service running as systemd, survives reboot
* Supabase audit_logs receiving resource snapshots every 60 seconds
* 90% cgroup warning fires correctly in #ceo (test by simulating high memory)
* Pre-deployment check blocks service start when headroom < 2GB

## Owner

Atlas — infrastructure. Concurrent with [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) (Weaviate install).

title: KEI-53 — Discovery validation governance: tiered CONCUR before permanent indexing (anti-epistemic-poisoning)
description:
## Problem: Epistemic Poisoning

A flawed discovery log written by Agent A gets indexed as permanent Layer 3 knowledge. Agent B pulls it as fact during bd claim context injection. The hallucination propagates as institutional memory.

## Fix: Tiered validation before permanent indexing

All discoveries enter a `staging` collection first. Promotion to `permanent` collection depends on the tier.

### Tier 1 — Routine technical finding

Definition: Verifiable in < 5 minutes by any agent (error codes, command outcomes, file paths, API behaviours)

Path: staging → auto-promotes to permanent after 24h if unchallenged
Challenge mechanism: any agent can run `bd challenge [discovery_id]` with a counter-finding — triggers Tier 2 review

### Tier 2 — Architecture-level discovery

Definition: Claims about system design, approach selection, "use X instead of Y" decisions

Path: staging → requires CONCUR from one peer agent before permanent
If no CONCUR within 48h: expires from staging, not promoted
Challenge mechanism: peer CONCUR/BLOCK on the finding

### Tier 3 — Contradicts a ratified decision

Definition: Discovery that contradicts a `ceo:rule:*` or ratified KEI decision

Path: staging → Slack notification to Dave → human approval required
Auto-expires in staging after 72h without approval

## Discovery log additions

```json
{
  "validation_tier": 1 | 2 | 3,
  "context_version": "KEI-44 · Cognee v0.7.3 · 2026-05-13",
  "status": "staging | permanent | expired | challenged"
}
```

## Staleness handling

Discoveries older than 90 days appear in bd claim injection with a flag:
"⚠ Discovery from \[date\] — verify still applies before relying on it"

No auto-deletion. Visibility only. Human or agent can explicitly re-verify and re-timestamp.

## Governance trace in Weaviate

Every discovery object stores: written_by, written_at, validated_by (if Tier 2+), promoted_at, challenged_by (if applicable). Full audit trail.

## Acceptance criteria

* staging and permanent collections exist in Weaviate
* Tier 1 auto-promotion runs on 24h schedule
* Tier 2 CONCUR gate implemented — peer must explicitly validate
* Tier 3 Slack notification fires to Dave on discovery creation
* Staleness flag appears in bd claim injection for discoveries > 90 days
* `bd challenge [id]` command implemented

## Owner

Aiden — governance implementation. Runs in parallel with [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from)/48.

title: KEI-51 — Agent profiles: personalised bd ready queue with capability affinity scoring
description:
## What

Add an `agent_profiles` table to Supabase that stores capability weights and context history per agent. Used by `bd ready --agent=[callsign]` to return a personalised priority queue rather than the same global list for all agents.

## Why

All agents currently see the same ordered KEI list. Max and Atlas have different strengths and different current context. A personalised queue means Max sees Cognee-related KEIs ranked higher, Atlas sees infrastructure KEIs higher. Same atomic claiming underneath — better routing on top.

## Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE agent_profiles (
  callsign TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  context_tags TEXT[],        -- e.g. ['cognee', 'memory', 'python']
  recent_kei_types TEXT[],    -- last 10 KEI tag sets completed
  capability_weights JSONB,   -- e.g. {"cognee": 0.9, "systemd": 0.3}
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
```

## Initial profiles (based on today's session)

```
elliot:  orchestration=0.9, governance=0.9, python=0.8, cognee=0.6
aiden:   code_review=0.9, refactoring=0.8, python=0.8, ci=0.7
max:     cognee=0.9, memory=0.9, python=0.7, systemd=0.3
atlas:   systemd=0.9, infrastructure=0.8, python=0.6, cognee=0.3
orion:   parallel_build=0.8, python=0.7, infrastructure=0.7
scout:   research=0.9, analysis=0.8, documentation=0.7
```

## Personalised bd ready query

```python
def bd_ready(agent_callsign):
    profile = get_agent_profile(agent_callsign)
    keis = get_unclaimed_unblocked_keis()
  
    # Apply affinity re-ranking
    for kei in keis:
        affinity = calculate_affinity(kei.tags, profile.capability_weights)
        kei.personalised_score = kei.priority_score * (1 + 0.5 * affinity)
  
    return sorted(keis, key=lambda k: k.personalised_score, reverse=True)
```

## Auto-update

When `bd complete` fires: update `recent_kei_types` with completed KEI's tags. Recalculate `capability_weights` using exponential moving average. Profile evolves automatically as agents complete work.

## Acceptance criteria

* `agent_profiles` table created in Supabase
* Initial profiles seeded for all 6 agents
* `bd ready --agent=max` returns different ordering than `bd ready --agent=atlas`
* Profiles update automatically on `bd complete`
* Soft preferred_agent hint: 1.5× boost if KEI has preferred_agent matching callsign, removed after 30 min if agent unavailable

## Dependencies
